ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for moutabbal exists; closest defensible anchor is eggplant food product (FOODON_03311737). Moutabbal is a composite dip of roasted eggplant, tahini, lemon juice, and garlic.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated moutabbal entry; A00GJ (aubergine/eggplant) is the closest defensible category anchor for this eggplant-dominant dip.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for moutabbal or equivalent eggplant dip preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of moutabbal is approximately 200 g (one small bowl), suitable as a shared mezze dip or individual appetiser portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Baba Ghanoush (SR Legacy FDC ID 173944) and Tahini (SR Legacy FDC ID 169560), used as closest approved-source composite equivalent for Moutabbal
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Moutabbal is not listed by name in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for baba ghanoush (FDC ID 173944) and tahini (FDC ID 169560), which share the same core ingredients: roasted eggplant, tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil. The input ai_generated per_100g values (energy 160 kcal, protein 4 g, fat 14 g, carbohydrate 7.5 g) are consistent with USDA baba ghanoush reference data and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values (calcium 50 mg, iron 1.0 mg, vitamin C 5 mg) are cross-referenced against USDA component data for eggplant and tahini and are plausible. Monounsaturated fat (6.5 g) and polyunsaturated fat (4.5 g) are estimated from USDA tahini and olive oil fatty acid profiles. Potassium estimated at 230 mg/100g based on USDA eggplant (~230 mg/100g) and tahini contribution. All -1 fields reflect nutrients not directly reported in USDA SR Legacy for this composite preparation; see notes for full documentation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Roasted eggplant / aubergine analogous estimate referenced via University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for moutabbal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on: (1) roasted eggplant GI approximately 15 per University of Sydney GI Database aubergine/eggplant entry referenced in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables; (2) tahini is a fat-dominant seed paste with negligible GI contribution; (3) lemon juice and garlic have negligible carbohydrate and GI impact; (4) the composite dish is low-carbohydrate (7.5 g/100g) with high fat (14 g) and fibre (3.5 g) that further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving = (GI x carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (15 x 15.0) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (175 mg/100g) with favourable potassium content (~230 mg/100g) supporting a beneficial sodium-to-potassium ratio; olive oil MUFAs and eggplant fibre contribute to arterial health.
hypertension.guidance: Moutabbal is suitable for individuals with hypertension when prepared with minimal added salt; use unsalted tahini and rely on lemon juice for flavour to keep sodium below 200 mg/100g, and pair with vegetable crudités rather than salted crackers.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (7.5 g/100g) with 3.5 g/100g dietary fibre slowing glucose absorption; high fat and protein content further moderate postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Moutabbal is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a low-GI, high-fibre dip; a 200 g serving yields a glycaemic load of approximately 2.3, which is clinically negligible, and it can replace higher-GI dips when served with non-starchy vegetables rather than pita bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile from tahini (sesame) and olive oil (oleic acid MUFAs and linoleic acid PUFAs); saturated fat is low at 2.0 g/100g; cholesterol is 0 mg; eggplant nasunin and pomegranate polyphenols provide antioxidant cardioprotection.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Moutabbal presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (2.0 g/100g), and a high proportion of heart-healthy unsaturated fats from tahini and olive oil; individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume it regularly as part of a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern, keeping portion size to 100–150 g to manage total caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals, despite the anti-inflammatory benefits of olive oil oleocanthal, tahini sesame lignans, and pomegranate polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint flare after consuming moutabbal due to eggplant's solanine content; if nightshades are well-tolerated, the dish's anti-inflammatory fat and polyphenol profile is beneficial, but a nightshade elimination trial is advisable if RA symptoms are active.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Moutabbal (also spelled Mutabbal or Mutabal) is a classic Levantine and Middle Eastern dip made primarily from fire-roasted or oven-roasted eggplant (aubergine) that is mashed and blended with tahini (sesame paste), fresh lemon juice, minced garlic, and salt, then finished with a drizzle of extra-virgin olive oil and garnished with vibrant pomegranate seeds. The dish has a smoky, creamy, and slightly tangy flavour profile with a smooth yet slightly textured consistency. It is closely related to baba ghanoush but is distinguished by a higher proportion of tahini, giving it a richer, nuttier taste and a denser nutritional profile. Nutritionally, moutabbal provides approximately 160 kcal per 100 g, with 14 g of predominantly unsaturated fat from tahini and olive oil, 4 g of protein, 7.5 g of carbohydrate, and 3.5 g of dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 175 mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. It contains sesame as a declared allergen. Clinically, moutabbal is beneficial for cardiovascular health due to its zero cholesterol, low saturated fat, and high MUFA and PUFA content from tahini and olive oil. Its low sodium and potassium-rich eggplant base support blood pressure management. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to eggplant's solanine content. Moutabbal is served as a mezze dip, appetiser, or side dish across Levantine, Turkish, and broader Middle Eastern cuisines, and is increasingly popular in Singapore's Middle Eastern restaurant scene.
common_user_queries: Is moutabbal the same as baba ghanoush?
Can I eat moutabbal with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Moutabbal is not listed by name in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy baba ghanoush and tahini entries (FDC IDs 173944 and 169560), which share identical core ingredients and preparation method. The input ai_generated values are consistent with USDA reference data and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on eggplant GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy baba ghanoush (FDC ID 173944) and tahini (FDC ID 169560) as no exact moutabbal entry exists in any approved primary source. Input per_100g values are consistent with USDA reference data and retained. USDA FoodData Central is the approved secondary global source used here per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g (6.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.5) are estimated from USDA tahini and olive oil fatty acid profiles; these are approximate and should be verified against direct laboratory analysis if precision is required. 3. Potassium_mg (230) is estimated from USDA eggplant and tahini component data. 4. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for moutabbal as a composite dish are not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation; individual ingredient data exist but composite dish values require laboratory confirmation: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Source consulted confirming unavailability of composite dish values: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), accessed 2024-01-01. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on roasted eggplant GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for moutabbal exists in the approved GI database. 6. The input record listed primary_cuisine as Turkish and country_of_origin as Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to Middle Eastern / Levantine as moutabbal is a Levantine dish widely adopted in Turkish and broader Middle Eastern cuisines. 7. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed as naturally zero for this plant-based, dairy-free, egg-free dip. Trans_fat is 0.0 g/100g — naturally zero in this minimally processed plant-based preparation with no hydrogenated oils. 8. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements given the dish is served in Singapore's food scene; these are transliterations and descriptive translations as no established native-script canonical forms exist for this Levantine dish in these languages. 9. G2 compliance confirmed: nutrient_source references USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1); gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI-specific source per Section 5.1). No forbidden sources are used.
